message:
test(cell): add 40 edge-case tests for Cell, CellContent, CellAttrs, PackedRgba (bd-35ddr)

Edge cases covered:
- PackedRgba: non-commutativity of over(), near-opaque alpha, both-partial-alpha
  blending, NaN/Infinity opacity clamping, alpha monotonicity sweep, associativity
  bounds, channel isolation, hash consistency
- CellContent: NUL char equals EMPTY, max Unicode U+10FFFF, BMP boundary chars,
  grapheme with zero/max width, CONTINUATION value invariant
- CellAttrs: chained mutations, all flags + max link_id, link_id masking
- Cell: PartialEq/bits_eq equivalence, grapheme content, with_char on continuation,
  size/alignment invariants, builder chain coverage
- Porter-Duff: reference implementation sweep across all alpha values

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1rzr","title":"Action Timeline: Alert Threshold Calibration (Conformal + e-process)","description":"## Goal
Set change-point alert thresholds with formal false-positive control and provide an explanation ledger per alert.

## Core Idea (Alien Artifact)
Conformal thresholding on residuals from the sketch baseline:
- Given calibration residuals R = {r_1..r_n}, threshold q = quantile_{1-alpha} using (n+1) rule.
- Alert when r_t > q.

Anytime-valid layer:
- Maintain an e-process on residuals to allow early alerts without inflating false positives.
- Example: e_t = exp(lambda * (r_t - mu) - (lambda^2 * sigma^2)/2), E_t = product e_t.
- Trigger if E_t > 1/alpha.

## Acceptance Criteria
- FPR <= alpha on synthetic and recorded traces.
- Alert carries evidence ledger: residual, q, conformal score, e-value, alpha.
- No alert spam under steady-state traffic.

## Tests (REQUIRED)
- unit_conformal_threshold: threshold equals (1-alpha) quantile of calibration set.
- unit_evalue_monotone: e-process nonnegative and grows with evidence.
- property_fpr_control: empirical FPR <= alpha + tolerance.

## E2E (REQUIRED)
- Replay trace with known change points; log alert timing and evidence.

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.

 {"id":"bd-2rs","title":"Implement clipboard integration (OSC 52)","description":"PHASE 7 EXTRAS - CLIPBOARD INTEGRATION

## Background
OSC 52 allows setting the system clipboard from terminal applications. This enables copy/paste functionality without external dependencies.

## Technical Requirements

### OSC 52 Protocol
Sequence: ESC ] 52 ; c ; <base64-data> BEL

c = clipboard selection:
- c = clipboard
- p = primary (X11)
- s = secondary
- 0-7 = cut buffers

### Clipboard API

pub struct Clipboard {
    caps: TerminalCapabilities,
}

impl Clipboard {
    /// Check if OSC 52 is supported
    pub fn is_available(&self) -> bool {
        self.caps.osc52
    }
    
    /// Set clipboard content
    pub fn set(&self, content: &str, writer: &mut impl Write) -> io::Result<()> {
        if !self.is_available() {
            return Err(io::Error::new(
                io::ErrorKind::Unsupported,
                "OSC 52 not available"
            ));
        }
        
        let encoded = base64::encode(content);
        write!(writer, "\x1b]52;c;{}\x07", encoded)?;
        writer.flush()
    }
    
    /// Clear clipboard
    pub fn clear(&self, writer: &mut impl Write) -> io::Result<()> {
        write!(writer, "\x1b]52;c;\x07")?;
        writer.flush()
    }
}

### Terminal Compatibility
OSC 52 support varies:
- ✅ iTerm2, kitty, alacritty, WezTerm, foot
- ⚠️ tmux (needs set-clipboard on)
- ⚠️ screen (needs clipboard support)
- ❌ Many terminal emulators disable by default

### Detection Strategy
1. Check known terminal from TERM/TERM_PROGRAM
2. Check tmux/screen allow-passthrough
3. Attempt set and check for response (risky)
4. Default: assume unsupported

### Size Limits
Most terminals limit OSC 52 payload:
- Common limit: 74994 bytes (base64)
- Split large content or fail gracefully

### Fallback Options
When OSC 52 unavailable:
- Fall back to external clipboard tool (feature: clipboard-fallback)
  - macOS: pbcopy/pbpaste
  - Linux: xclip, xsel, wl-copy
  - Windows: clip.exe
- Or return error for application to handle

## Tests Required
- [ ] OSC 52 sequence generation
- [ ] Base64 encoding correct
- [ ] Size limit handling
- [ ] Clear clipboard works
- [ ] Capability detection
- [ ] Fallback to external tools
- [ ] Error handling when unavailable

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] OSC 52 set clipboard
- [ ] Capability detection
- [ ] Size limit enforcement
- [ ] Clear clipboard
- [ ] Optional external fallback
- [ ] Feature gate: clipboard

 {"id":"bd-2ryo","title":"Update shared helper functions to use Frame","description":"## Scope

Update the shared helper functions in ftui-widgets/src/lib.rs that are used
by multiple widgets.

## Functions to Update

Based on the error output, these functions need updating:

1. **draw_text_span** (lib.rs:104)
   - Currently: `fn draw_text_span(frame: &mut Frame, ...)`
   - Callers still pass `buf` instead of `frame`
   - Either: Update all callers to pass frame
   - Or: Change function to take `buf: &mut Buffer` if it doesn't need Frame features

2. **set_style_area** (lib.rs:87)
   - Takes `buf: &mut Buffer` - this is correct for styling operations
   - Callers may need to pass `&mut frame.buffer`

3. **apply_style** (lib.rs:66)
   - Takes `cell: &mut Cell` - unchanged, works at cell level

## Design Decision

**For helper functions that don't need Frame features:**
Keep them taking `&mut Buffer` and have callers pass `&mut frame.buffer`.
This is cleaner than passing Frame unnecessarily.

**For helper functions that DO need Frame features:**
Update to take `&mut Frame` and update all callers.

## Analysis Required

For each helper:
1. Does it need hit_grid? → needs Frame
2. Does it need cursor? → needs Frame
3. Does it need degradation? → needs Frame
4. None of the above? → keep Buffer

- `cargo check -p ftui-widgets` must pass
- No function signature mismatches","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T23:23:00.987192527Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T23:25:27.639400452Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T23:25:27.639380665Z","close_reason":"Helper functions updated: draw_text_span now takes Frame, set_style_area and apply_style kept taking Buffer/Cell (no Frame features needed).  {"id":"bd-2sog","title":"Demo Showcase: Terminal Capability Explorer","description":"## Background

Terminal capability detection is messy:
- TERM lies
- multiplexers change behavior
- color support varies
- sync output may or may not exist

FrankenTUI already does capability detection conservatively. This screen makes those decisions visible and debuggable, and turns them into a compelling demo.

## UX / Core Interactions

- Capability matrix view:
  - detected capability: yes/no/unknown
  - confidence (if modeled)
  - evidence (TERM/COLORTERM/tty, known terminal list, etc.)
  - fallback behavior currently active

- Optional "what if" panel:
  - allow simulating capability profiles (ties into bd-k4lj)
  - show how behavior changes (e.g., sync output on/off)

Suggested keybindings:
- Tab: switch views (matrix / evidence ledger / simulation)
- Enter: expand details for a capability

## Architecture Notes

- Data source: ftui-core terminal capability detection.
- Export: ability to emit the current capability set as JSON (useful for bug reports).
- Keep presentation deterministic; do not rely on unstable environment ordering.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Displays all major capabilities FrankenTUI cares about (mouse, focus, color depth, sync output, etc.).
- For each capability, provides:
  - the current decision
  - the fallback behavior
  - a human-readable reason

Additionally (project-wide standards):
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures; add property tests for core invariants where applicable
- E2E: scripts with verbose JSONL logging (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums) + deterministic mode

## Alien Artifact Upgrades (Concrete)

- Evidence ledger view:
  - record evidence contributions and show a compact explanation per capability
- Bayesian framing (optional):
  - represent capability detection as a posterior over states
  - use explicit loss to decide conservative enable/disable

## Extreme Optimization

- Rendering this screen must be cheap (it should not distort the very metrics it displays).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-02T22:31:03.651248923Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T18:23:11.543433560Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T18:23:11.543415276Z","close_reason":"Terminal Capability Explorer screen implemented + demo wiring + snapshot coverage; follow-on tasks tracked in bd-2sog.*","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["debug","demo","ux"],"comments":[{"id":379,"issue_id":"bd-2sog","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Scope

 {"id":"bd-3vbf","title":"Visual Polish Epic: Transform Demo Showcase into Jaw-Dropping Experience","description":"## Epic Overview

Transform the FrankenTUI demo-showcase from 'functional demonstration' to 'jaw-droppingly impressive work of art' - every section should be a visual masterpiece that showcases what's possible in terminal UI.

## Inspiration Sources

1. **beads_viewer** (/dp/beads_viewer) - Famous for looking amazing
   - Sophisticated semantic color system with status/priority/type badges
   - Pre-computed styles for performance and consistency
   - Micro-visualizations (sparklines, mini-bars, heatmaps)
   - Rich iconography with emoji/symbols
   - Multi-view modes with smooth transitions
   - WCAG AA compliant contrast ratios

2. **charmed_rust** (/dp/charmed_rust) - Already-ported Charmbracelet components
   - lipgloss: CSS-like declarative styling
   - bubbles: Rich UI components (spinner, textinput, progress, etc.)
   - harmonica: Spring physics for smooth animations
   - glamour: Markdown rendering with syntax highlighting

## Goals

1. **Design System Foundation** - Semantic colors, spacing tokens, pre-computed styles
2. **Chrome Polish** - Tab bar, status bar, help overlay improvements
3. **Screen-by-Screen Excellence** - Each demo screen is a work of art
4. **New Capabilities** - Add missing features that beads_viewer/charmed_rust have
5. **Micro-interactions** - Subtle animations, smooth transitions, polish
6. **Accessibility** - WCAG AA compliance, graceful degradation

## Success Criteria

- Visitors say 'wow' when they see the demo
- Each screen could be a standalone showcase
- Colors convey meaning, not just decoration
- Animations feel natural and purposeful
- Performance remains excellent

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.
- Color: operate in perceptual space (OKLCH/CIELAB) with hard contrast constraints; precompute palettes via constrained optimization and prove WCAG bounds in tests.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.


## E2E Plan
- Provide/extend an E2E script that exercises the feature end-to-end in PTY (where applicable).
- Logging: verbose JSONL with env/capabilities + per-step timings + seed + checksums; include a summary at end.
- On failure: print reproduction command and point to artifacts directory.

 {"id":"bd-3vbf.1","title":"Design System: Semantic Color Palette with Status/Priority/Type Colors","description":"## Background

The beads_viewer achieves its polished look through a sophisticated color system where **every color conveys meaning**:
- Status colors: OPEN (green), IN_PROGRESS (blue), BLOCKED (red), CLOSED (muted)
- Priority colors: P0/CRITICAL (hot red), P1/HIGH (orange), P2/MEDIUM (yellow), P3/LOW (cyan)
- Type colors: Bug (red), Feature (purple), Task (blue), Epic (gold)

Each semantic color has:
- Primary foreground color
- Background tint for badges/highlights
- Contrast-validated text color for backgrounds
- Pre-computed Style objects for performance

Currently FrankenTUI's theme.rs has basic colors but lacks semantic meaning.

## Implementation

1. Add semantic color modules to theme.rs:
```rust
pub mod status {
    pub const OPEN: PackedRgba = PackedRgba::rgb(80, 220, 140);
    pub const OPEN_BG: PackedRgba = PackedRgba::rgba(80, 220, 140, 40);
    pub const IN_PROGRESS: PackedRgba = PackedRgba::rgb(100, 180, 255);
    pub const BLOCKED: PackedRgba = PackedRgba::rgb(255, 100, 100);
    pub const CLOSED: PackedRgba = PackedRgba::rgb(120, 120, 140);
}

pub mod priority {
    pub const CRITICAL: PackedRgba = PackedRgba::rgb(255, 50, 50);
    pub const HIGH: PackedRgba = PackedRgba::rgb(255, 150, 50);
    pub const MEDIUM: PackedRgba = PackedRgba::rgb(255, 220, 80);
    pub const LOW: PackedRgba = PackedRgba::rgb(100, 200, 255);
}
```

2. Create SemanticStyles struct with pre-computed styles
3. Add contrast validation utility
4. Document color meanings

## Justification

Colors are the foundation of visual design. Semantic colors:
- Reduce cognitive load (users learn color meanings)
- Create visual hierarchy
- Enable instant recognition of status/priority
- Make the UI feel professional and intentional

## Files to Modify

- crates/ftui-demo-showcase/src/theme.rs

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Status colors defined (open/progress/blocked/closed)
- [ ] Priority colors defined (P0-P4)
- [ ] Type colors defined (error/warning/info/success)
- [ ] Background tint variants for each
- [ ] Contrast ratios >= 4.5:1 (WCAG AA)
- [ ] Pre-computed SemanticStyles available","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-02T21:41:05.364954759Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-02T22:52:04.625588117Z","closed_at":"2026-02-02T22:52:04.625568560Z","close_reason":"Implemented semantic palette + contrast utilities + SemanticStyles","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["polish","showcase","theme"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3vbf.1","depends_on_id":"bd-3vbf","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T14:32:18Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":446,"issue_id":"bd-3vbf.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Unit Tests Required

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod semantic_color_tests {
    use super::*;
    
    #[test]
    fn status_colors_have_valid_contrast() {
        // WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio
        fn contrast_ratio(fg: PackedRgba, bg: PackedRgba) -> f64 {
            let lum_fg = relative_luminance(fg);
            let lum_bg = relative_luminance(bg);
            let lighter = lum_fg.max(lum_bg);
            let darker = lum_fg.min(lum_bg);
            (lighter + 0.05) / (darker + 0.05)
        }
        
        fn relative_luminance(c: PackedRgba) -> f64 {
            let r = srgb_to_linear(c.r() as f64 / 255.0);
            let g = srgb_to_linear(c.g() as f64 / 255.0);
            let b = srgb_to_linear(c.b() as f64 / 255.0);
            0.2126 * r + 0.7152 * g + 0.0722 * b
        }
        
        fn srgb_to_linear(c: f64) -> f64 {
            if c <= 0.03928 { c / 12.92 } else { ((c + 0.055) / 1.055).powf(2.4) }
        }
        
        assert!(contrast_ratio(status::OPEN, bg::BASE) >= 4.5);
        assert!(contrast_ratio(status::BLOCKED, bg::BASE) >= 4.5);
        assert!(contrast_ratio(status::PROGRESS, bg::BASE) >= 4.5);
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn all_priority_colors_distinct() {
        let colors = [priority::CRITICAL, priority::HIGH, priority::MEDIUM, priority::LOW];
        for (i, c1) in colors.iter().enumerate() {
            for (j, c2) in colors.iter().enumerate() {
                if i != j {
                    assert_ne!(c1, c2);
                }
            }
        }
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn status_bg_variants_have_low_opacity() {
        // Background variants should have alpha < 128 for tinting
        assert!(status::OPEN_BG.a() < 128);
        assert!(status::BLOCKED_BG.a() < 128);
    }
}
```

 {"id":"bd-3vbf.10","title":"Data Viz: Add Sparklines Widget for Trend Visualization","description":"## Background

beads_viewer uses sparklines extensively for showing trends:
- 8-level Unicode block characters: ▁▂▃▄▅▆▇█
- Auto-scaling with min/max detection
- Color gradients from cold to hot
- Compact visualization in tight spaces

Sparklines are essential for data-rich dashboards.

## Current State

charts.rs has chart implementations but sparklines may not be fully featured.
charmed_rust has sparkline support that could be referenced.

## Implementation

1. Sparkline widget in ftui-widgets or ftui-extras:
```rust
pub struct Sparkline<'a> {
    data: &'a [f64],
    min: Option<f64>,
    max: Option<f64>,
    color: PackedRgba,
    gradient: Option<(PackedRgba, PackedRgba)>,
}

impl Sparkline<'_> {
    const CHARS: &'static [char] = &[' ', '▁', '▂', '▃', '▄', '▅', '▆', '▇', '█'];
    
    pub fn render_string(&self) -> String {
        let min = self.min.unwrap_or_else(|| self.data.iter().copied().fold(f64::INFINITY, f64::min));
        let max = self.max.unwrap_or_else(|| self.data.iter().copied().fold(f64::NEG_INFINITY, f64::max));
        let range = max - min;
        
        self.data.iter().map(|&v| {
            let normalized = if range > 0.0 { (v - min) / range } else { 0.5 };
            let idx = (normalized * 8.0).round() as usize;
            Self::CHARS[idx.min(8)]
        }).collect()
    }
}
```

- CI/gates run `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`.
- `crates/ftui-extras/src/theme.rs` contains edge-case unit tests that intentionally exercise trait coverage (Debug/Hash/Copy/Clone), but several tests currently call `.clone()` on types that are `Copy`, triggering `clippy::clone_on_copy` under `-D warnings`.

Why This Matters
- This blocks the primary quality gate for the whole workspace.
- We want to keep the *coverage intent* of the tests without disabling clippy or weakening lint standards.

Scope
- Update only the affected tests in `crates/ftui-extras/src/theme.rs` so they remain meaningful while being clippy-clean.
- Do NOT relax lint settings. Do NOT delete tests.

Implementation Notes
- Replace `let c = a.clone();` with `let c = a;` (Copy assignment) and keep the semantic assertions.
- Add a short comment where helpful: `// Copy implies Clone; clippy forbids clone_on_copy`.
- If explicit Clone-trait coverage is still desired, prefer a compile-time bound assertion over runtime `.clone()` calls (but note: `Copy: Clone`, so this is typically redundant).

 {"id":"bd-lff4p.8","title":"Spec: Correctness strategy for terminal engines (invariants, fuzz, golden traces)","description":"Goal
- Specify correctness guarantees strong enough to justify replacing BOTH Crossterm and xterm.js.

Correctness Model
- Treat the terminal engine as a deterministic state machine driven only by explicit inputs:
  - output byte streams (VT/ANSI) and/or patches
  - input events (keyboard/mouse/touch/IME)
  - resize events
  - ticks/time (explicitly injected; never implicit global time)

Must Include
- Formal-ish invariants (written down, then enforced):
  - parser state machine safety (no panic, bounded memory growth, valid transitions)
  - grid mutations (cursor bounds, width invariants, no invalid placeholder cells)
  - scrollback correctness (capacity, wrap/reflow policy, viewport invariants)
  - selection semantics (spans scrollback+viewport; stable under resize where specified)
  - hyperlink semantics (OSC 8 lifetime + mapping)
- Proptest strategy
  - generators for byte streams, events, and resize storms
  - shrinking strategy that yields minimal repros
- Fuzzing strategy
  - entrypoints + corpora + crash triage protocol
  - every crash becomes a regression fixture
- Golden artifacts
  - conformance fixtures (byte stream -> expected grid snapshots)
  - golden trace corpus (event log -> expected checksums)
  - renderer goldens (framebuffer/patch checksums for key scenes)
- Differential testing (high-value)
  - compare FrankenTerm state against reference emulators for the same byte streams

E2E & Logging Requirements
- Native: `tests/e2e/scripts/*` must remain green and emit detailed JSONL.
- Web: a trace runner must replay the same golden traces and verify checksums.
- JSONL must conform to `tests/e2e/lib/e2e_jsonl_schema.json` (extend schema if needed, never break silently).
- Every run logs: run_id, build_id/git_sha, seed, size, caps/profile, perf counters, and checksums.

Acceptance Criteria
- A concrete test matrix mapping each user-facing feature to unit/property/fuzz/e2e coverage.
- CI hooks exist for: wasm build checks, proptest, fuzz smoke, trace replay, and checksum gates.
- A "never regress" list exists (copy/paste, selection, OSC8, resize, IME/composition, focus/mouse modes) with golden artifacts.

- Migration plan that avoids long-lived compatibility shims.

Acceptance Criteria
- A crisp, implementable interface + crate map.
- A staged migration plan with "delete old code" checkpoints (file deletion rule applies).
